Second-author paper · 2023

A method of quasi in-situ EBSD observation for microstructure and damage evolution in fatigue and dwell fatigue of Ti alloys

Chengqi Sun, Jian Sun, Weiqian Chi, Jiaxuan Wang, Wenjing Wang. International Journal of Fatigue 176 (2023) 107897.

Post-mortem microscopy shows the end state of fatigue damage, but not the sequence by which it formed. This work establishes a repeatable quasi in-situ EBSD procedure using a conventional MTS machine and returns to labelled regions after selected cycle counts. The resulting maps expose a competition between slip, twinning, grain refinement, microcrack nucleation, and dwell-induced plasticity.

DOI ↗ PDF ↓ Publisher

On this page

  1. Context

01 · Research context

Why this problem matters

Titanium pressure-hull and aero-engine components can experience maximum-stress dwell as well as cyclic loading. Dwell sensitivity is controlled not only by nominal stress and hold time, but also by crystallographic orientation and local deformation. Traditional fracture-surface analysis cannot reconstruct every stage of that microstructural evolution.

True in-situ EBSD fatigue experiments require specialized equipment and constrained specimen configurations. The paper addresses that experimental gap by combining conventional mechanical testing with repeat observations of indented, recoverable surface regions. The method is designed to retain crystallographic context while allowing realistic fatigue and dwell-fatigue waveforms.

02 · Scientific question

Core question

How do slip, deformation twinning, subgrain formation, and microcracking evolve in the same Ti-6Al-4V ELI grains as cyclic and dwell loading accumulate?

03 · Materials & methods

Connecting loading history to local evidence

The central method is temporal registration: mark a surface region, load the specimen for a known interval, remove and clean it, relocate the same grains, and repeat the EBSD/SEM observations.

Material

Forged and rolled Ti-6Al-4V ELI with equiaxed alpha and lamellar transformed-beta; specimen axis parallel to rolling direction.

Specimen

A 1 mm thick plate specimen with a polished observation face and labelled regions.

Loading system

MTS machine, axial load control, room-temperature air; triangular fatigue and trapezoidal dwell-fatigue waveforms.

Stress condition

Maximum stress 815 MPa and minimum stress 0 MPa for the three detailed specimens.

Compared histories

Fatigue: 10,686 cycles to fatigue failure. Dwell 10 s: 883 cycles to ductile failure. Dwell 120 s: 199 cycles to ductile failure.

Characterization

Repeated SEM and EBSD with BC, phase, IPF, KAM, twin-boundary, Schmid-factor, and misorientation analyses.

Registration workflow

Indent labelled regions, interrupt loading at selected cycles, remove and directionally clean the specimen, dry it, and relocate the same microstructure.

  1. Polish + label grains
  2. Baseline SEM / EBSD
  3. Controlled cyclic interval
  4. Relocate same region
  5. Map slip / twins / KAM
  6. Link evolution to failure mode

04 · Experimental design

Designing the comparison

All three principal tests used a maximum stress of 815 MPa, enabling the waveform and dwell duration to be compared without changing the peak level. The three histories produced markedly different lives and failure modes. The interrupted observations were concentrated both near and away from the final fracture surface, which helped separate distributed deformation from locally critical damage.

05 · Key findings

Evidence and interpretation

01

Slip activation followed crystallographic driving force, but dwell changed how broadly it developed.

Basal or prismatic slip tended to occur in alpha grains with larger Schmid factors for the corresponding systems. A moderate maximum-stress dwell promoted slip activation, but more deformation did not monotonically activate more slip systems. The 120 s dwell produced intense plasticity and short life rather than a simple extension of the 10 s response.

02

Twinning occupied a constrained crystallographic window.

Across fatigue and dwell fatigue, deformation twins appeared in alpha grains with prismatic-slip Schmid factors no greater than 0.2 and c-axis angles to the loading direction below 46°. Whether a capable grain actually twinned depended on both its orientation and the loading history. The 10 s dwell activated twinning in more eligible grains, while the longer 120 s dwell did not simply increase the twinned population.

03

Repeated EBSD captured subgrain formation instead of inferring it post mortem.

Misorientation increased and subgrains developed inside alpha grains under both loading types. The sequential maps show twin-related boundaries appearing and evolving as cycles accumulated. This directly supports dislocation sliding, dislocation interaction, and twinning as contributors to cyclic grain refinement.

04

Dwell changed both where microcracks nucleated and whether they could continue growing.

Under fatigue, microcracks tended to initiate on prismatic planes in alpha grains with larger prismatic-slip Schmid factors. Under dwell fatigue, initiation shifted toward basal planes with larger basal-slip factors or alpha-grain boundaries with small c-axis angles to the applied stress. A moderate dwell could favor brittle crack formation, but larger dwell-induced plastic strain could blunt or restrain brittle microcrack growth and produce a ductile failure mode.

05

Failure mode is the organizing variable for interpreting dwell fatigue.

At 815 MPa, conventional fatigue failed after 10,686 cycles with a fatigue-dominated fracture, whereas the 10 s and 120 s dwell specimens failed after 883 and 199 cycles with ductile failure. The paper argues that a crack-growth-only interpretation is inadequate when plastic deformation dominates or shares control of final failure.

06 · Failure mechanism

A staged interpretation

The observations support a competition model rather than a one-directional statement that dwell is always more damaging through the same pathway.

01

Orientation selects deformation

Schmid factors and c-axis alignment influence which grains accommodate basal slip, prismatic slip, or twinning.

02

Dwell redistributes plastic strain

Maximum-stress hold increases time-dependent deformation and can activate additional local slip or twinning.

03

Microstructure evolves

Misorientation accumulates; twin boundaries and dislocation structures subdivide alpha grains into subgrains.

04

Two competing crack effects

Moderate dwell can favor brittle crack nucleation, while larger accumulated plastic strain can restrain brittle microcrack advance.

05

Failure mode emerges

Fatigue, mixed, or ductile failure follows from the balance between crack initiation/growth and plastic-deformation-driven damage.

07 · My contribution

Verified authorship role

The paper's CRediT statement assigns Jian Sun visualization, formal analysis, investigation, and writing - review and editing. As second author, I contributed to the experimental evidence and its quantitative interpretation; the study's methodology, supervision, and original-draft leadership are not claimed as my sole responsibilities.

Verified from the paper's CRediT authorship contribution statement.

08 · Impact & future direction

What this work enables next

Research significance

The method converts EBSD from a terminal snapshot into a sequence of registered microstructural states using broadly available fatigue equipment. Scientifically, it shows why dwell-fatigue life and mechanism must be interpreted together with failure mode and local crystallography rather than through nominal loading parameters alone.

Boundaries of the evidence

Surface EBSD captures polished, observable regions and may not represent every subsurface event. Removing, cleaning, and remounting the specimen introduces practical registration limits. The detailed comparison uses one alloy, one maximum stress, and three specific loading histories; it does not by itself constitute a general dwell-fatigue law.

Doctoral research direction

The workflow could be coupled with digital image correlation and crystal-plasticity finite-element simulations built from measured grain maps. Such a framework would test whether local slip and twin evolution predict microcrack sites, and could inform microstructure-sensitive, probabilistic fatigue models for extreme-environment components.

Next case studyArtificial surface defects in very-high-cycle fatigue
